Goal G3: mission controller from corpus

Active goal, superseding the prose-model framing. Train a NASA-like controller whose communication, reasoning, delegation, risk sense, and mission-goal understanding come from its corpus, not from alignment layers added after training.

The target model behaves like a flight controller: operational register by default, disciplined structured outputs, and graceful degradation to plain conversation (a pilot asked what they ate for lunch, not a poet told to be terse). Its world is a mission simulator that holds all ground truth, and every training text is a translation of simulated state, events, decisions, and traffic. Prose-writing competence is no longer the goal; general language survives as a substrate tier and a first-class fragility gauge.

G3 supersedes the framing in which G1 (fluent referent-free prose) and G2 (referent-free reasoning) were ends in themselves; both survive as ingredients. The redirection was set after the grounded-corpus era showed both extremes fail: free LLM writing gave fluency with binding at chance, and a fully program-grounded corpus gave binding at 0.73-0.81 while collapsing general language to schema (the model answered a bare greeting with a fabricated fact). The controller target gives the built machinery its purpose: program-as-author becomes the simulator, the graph pipeline becomes the native state interface, and construction-solving asymmetry becomes doctrine adjudication. The register requirement is asymmetric by design: leakage of operational phrasing into casual chat is acceptable, verbose conversational prose in operational traffic is penalized. The falsifiable claim is transfer: whether logic learned in simulation (arithmetic, margins, decision rules) applies to real problems and real operational transcripts the model never trained on. The MVP, tiers, evaluation axes, and phase plan are recorded in docs/sgm_mvp.md; the notation is node 54, the architecture node 55, the corpus tiers node 56.
